class_schedule.topic: Globalization and the International Business Environment
learning_objectives: Define globalization and assess its drivers and consequences
Analyze the international trade and investment landscape
Evaluate the debate between globalization proponents and critics
readings: Ghemawat, Why the World Isn't Flat (HBR)

lecture_notes.introduction: International business encompasses all commercial transactions crossing national borders. Understanding the global environment is essential for any manager in today's interconnected economy.
key_concepts: Globalization drivers: technology, trade liberalization, capital flows
FDI patterns and multinational enterprise evolution
Ghemawat's CAGE distance framework: Cultural, Administrative, Geographic, Economic
Global vs. regional integration debate
Anti-globalization movements and reshoring trends
lecture_notes.demonstration: CAGE distance analysis comparing market entry difficulty for a US company entering Japan vs. Canada vs. Nigeria
setup_requirements: World Bank and WTO data access

review.value: Countries are separated by Cultural, Administrative, Geographic, and Economic distances that affect the attractiveness and difficulty of international business
Foreign Direct Investment: establishing a lasting interest in a foreign enterprise, distinct from portfolio investment

learning_objectives: Apply Hofstede's cultural dimensions to compare countries
Identify how culture affects management practices and negotiations
Develop strategies for managing cross-cultural teams

lecture_notes.introduction: Culture profoundly shapes how people communicate, make decisions, and do business. Cross-cultural competence is the most critical soft skill for international managers.
key_concepts: Hofstede's dimensions: power distance, individualism, masculinity, uncertainty avoidance, long-term orientation, indulgence
GLOBE study extensions and cultural clusters
High-context vs. low-context communication (Hall)
Cross-cultural negotiation styles
Managing multicultural teams: challenges and strategies
lecture_notes.demonstration: Role-play negotiation exercise between teams representing different cultural styles (US direct vs. Japanese indirect)

review.value: The degree to which less powerful members accept and expect unequal power distribution; affects hierarchy and decision-making styles
High-context cultures (Japan, China) rely on implicit communication and relationships; low-context (US, Germany) rely on explicit, direct communication

learning_objectives: Explain comparative advantage and its implications for trade
Analyze trade barriers: tariffs, quotas, and non-tariff barriers
Evaluate the role of WTO and regional trade agreements

lecture_notes.introduction: Trade theory explains why nations trade and the benefits of specialization. Trade policy determines the rules that govern international commerce.
key_concepts: Absolute advantage (Smith) vs. comparative advantage (Ricardo)
Heckscher-Ohlin factor endowment theory
New trade theory: economies of scale and first-mover advantage
Tariffs, quotas, subsidies, and non-tariff barriers
WTO, USMCA, EU, RCEP: trade governance architecture
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculating comparative advantage between two countries and demonstrating gains from trade

review.value: A country should specialize in producing goods where it has the lowest opportunity cost, even if it can produce everything more efficiently than trading partners
Tariffs (taxes on imports), quotas (quantity limits), subsidies (domestic producer support), and non-tariff barriers (regulations, standards)

learning_objectives: Compare entry modes: exporting, licensing, JVs, wholly owned subsidiaries
Evaluate the tradeoffs of control, risk, and resource commitment
Select appropriate entry strategies based on firm and market characteristics

lecture_notes.introduction: Choosing how to enter a foreign market is one of the most consequential strategic decisions a firm faces. Each entry mode involves different levels of risk, control, and commitment.
key_concepts: Exporting: direct vs. indirect, low risk but low control
Licensing and franchising: leveraging IP with limited investment
Joint ventures: shared risk, local knowledge, but potential conflicts
Wholly owned subsidiaries: greenfield vs. acquisition
Entry mode decision factors: country risk, cultural distance, firm resources
lecture_notes.demonstration: Decision matrix exercise selecting entry mode for Starbucks entering Saudi Arabia, considering cultural, legal, and strategic factors

review.value: From low commitment/low control (exporting) to high commitment/high control (wholly owned subsidiary); tradeoff between risk and reward
Two or more firms create a jointly owned entity; combines local knowledge with foreign firm capabilities but requires partner trust and alignment

class_schedule.topic: Global Strategy: Integration vs. Local Responsiveness
learning_objectives: Compare global, multidomestic, transnational, and international strategies
Analyze how firms balance standardization with adaptation

lecture_notes.introduction: The central tension in international strategy is balancing global efficiency (standardize everything) with local responsiveness (adapt everything). The best approach depends on industry and firm circumstances.
key_concepts: Pressures for global integration: cost reduction, universal needs
Pressures for local responsiveness: tastes, regulations, distribution
Four strategies: international, multidomestic, global standardization, transnational
Organizational structures for international strategy
Global value chain configuration and coordination
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping McDonald's strategy: which elements are globally standardized (brand, operations systems) vs. locally adapted (menu, marketing)

review.value: Simultaneously achieving global efficiency, local responsiveness, and worldwide learning; the most complex but most rewarding approach
Positions strategies on two axes: pressure for global integration (y-axis) and pressure for local responsiveness (x-axis)

learning_objectives: Explain exchange rate determination and currency risk management
Assess political and country risk for international operations
Evaluate ethical challenges in international business

lecture_notes.introduction: International business operates across currencies, political systems, and legal frameworks. Managing financial and political risks is essential for protecting international investments.
key_concepts: Exchange rate systems: fixed, floating, managed float
Currency risk: transaction, translation, and economic exposure
Hedging instruments: forwards, futures, options, swaps
Political risk: expropriation, policy instability, corruption
Ethical relativism vs. universalism in global business
lecture_notes.demonstration: Currency risk scenario: calculating the impact of a 15% currency depreciation on a multinational's overseas earnings and designing a hedging strategy

review.value: Risk that exchange rate changes between transaction agreement and settlement will affect the value in home currency
The probability that political decisions, events, or conditions will affect the profitability or sustainability of an international investment

questions.question: A US tech company plans to expand to Japan. Using Hofstede's cultural dimensions, identify two specific management practices the company should adapt and explain why.
questions.answer: 1) Decision-making: Japan scores high on uncertainty avoidance, so the company should expect and accommodate more thorough decision-making processes (nemawashi/ringi) rather than the fast, top-down decisions common in US firms. Rushing decisions will encounter resistance. 2) Team dynamics: Japan scores high on collectivism, so the company should emphasize team-based goals and rewards rather than individual recognition programs. Singling out individuals for praise can cause discomfort in a group-oriented culture.

rubric.key: Clear reasoning linking dimension to practice

questions.question: Country A can produce 100 units of wine or 50 units of cloth. Country B can produce 60 units of wine or 40 units of cloth. Which country has comparative advantage in wine? Show your reasoning.
questions.answer: Country A opportunity cost of 1 wine = 50/100 = 0.5 cloth. Country B opportunity cost of 1 wine = 40/60 = 0.67 cloth. Country A has comparative advantage in wine because its opportunity cost (0.5 cloth) is lower than Country B's (0.67 cloth). Country B has comparative advantage in cloth (1 wine/0.67 = 1.5 wine per cloth for B vs. 2 wine per cloth for A).

questions.question: Why might a firm choose a joint venture over a wholly owned subsidiary when entering a politically unstable country, even if the firm has sufficient resources for full ownership?
questions.answer: In politically unstable countries, a JV with a local partner provides: (1) risk sharing, limiting financial exposure if political events disrupt operations; (2) local legitimacy and government connections that reduce the risk of hostile government action against a foreign-owned entity; (3) local knowledge for navigating bureaucratic and regulatory complexities; (4) a local partner with political stakes who can advocate for the venture's interests. The reduced control is a worthwhile tradeoff for reduced political risk exposure.

questions.question: IKEA uses largely the same product designs worldwide but adapts store layouts, product sizes, and marketing to local markets. Is this a global standardization strategy or transnational strategy? Justify your answer.
questions.answer: This is a transnational strategy. IKEA simultaneously pursues global efficiency through standardized designs and supply chain (high integration pressure for cost reduction) while adapting to local market needs (local responsiveness in marketing and some product features). A pure global standardization strategy would not adapt store layouts or product sizes. IKEA's approach balances both pressures, which is the hallmark of the transnational strategy, achieving cost efficiency through global scale while remaining responsive to local preferences.

major_assignments.title: International Market Entry Plan
major_assignments.description: Develop a comprehensive market entry plan for a real company entering a specific foreign market. Include country analysis, entry mode selection, international strategy, and risk assessment with mitigation strategies.
objectives: Apply international business frameworks to a real market entry scenario
Conduct thorough country and competitive analysis
Select and justify an entry mode strategy
Assess and plan for political, cultural, and financial risks
deliverables: Country analysis using CAGE and PESTEL frameworks
Entry mode analysis with decision rationale
Risk assessment matrix with mitigation strategies

criteria: CAGE distance analysis completed for target country
PESTEL factors specific to target market identified
Market size and growth data sourced and interpreted
Cultural analysis using Hofstede dimensions
Multiple entry modes evaluated with pros and cons
Selected entry mode well-justified given analysis
Strategy (global, multidomestic, transnational) clearly articulated
Competitive positioning in target market defined
Political, economic, and cultural risks identified
Currency risk quantified and hedging strategy proposed
Risk mitigation strategies are practical and specific
Scenario analysis for major risk events
Professional consulting-quality format
Data from credible international sources
Clear recommendations with supporting logic
Executive summary provided
Thorough country analysis using international frameworks
Well-justified entry mode and strategy selection
Comprehensive risk assessment with mitigation plans
Professional report quality with credible data sources

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of international business concepts and frameworks

case_discussions.description: Active participation in international business case study discussions

midterm_examination.description: Covers globalization, culture, trade theory, and entry strategies

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including global strategy, finance, and risk

course_policies.attendance: Attendance mandatory for case discussions. International guest speaker sessions are especially valued.
course_policies.late_submissions: Market entry plan penalized 10% per day. No late submissions accepted for case analyses.
course_policies.collaboration: Market entry plan may be completed in teams of 2-3. Quizzes and exams are individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All country data must be from verifiable sources. Copying from consulting reports without attribution is prohibited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to international databases: World Bank, IMF, UNCTAD, Hofstede Insights. Presentation software.
